title: Single Parent After Divorce: Rebuilding Financial Security
goals: Secure income protection for children if Jennifer dies or is disabled
constraints: Child custody costs and legal expenses
key_risks: Life insurance gap: $50K coverage vs $1.2M need with two dependents
Disability would be catastrophic with no backup income
Inadequate emergency fund for unexpected car/home expenses
Retirement derailment from extended divorce process

planning_issues.description: Life insurance: $50K employer coverage vs $1.2M need (15x income as single parent); disability income insurance missing
2.1 months emergency fund — below 3-month minimum; single-income households should target 6 months
Only $550/month surplus leaves no room for savings or unexpected expenses
$62,000 at age 42 — below target for income level; contributions paused during divorce

key_recommendations: Add own-occupation disability to protect nursing income
Name children's guardian as beneficiary trustee in updated will
Rebuild emergency fund to $25,000 over 18 months
Resume 401k contributions at minimum to capture employer match
follow_up_questions: Is ex-spouse court-ordered to carry life insurance for child support?
What is the custody arrangement — shared custody affects financial aid and dependent care
Does Jennifer's nursing employer offer group disability insurance?
Is child support or alimony taxable income for Jennifer?
outcome_summary: Purchased $1.2M term life policy and disability insurance within 60 days. Will and guardianship documents updated. Emergency fund rebuilt to $18,000 in 12 months.
key_lesson: Single parents face concentrated financial risk — all income and childcare depends on one person. Life and disability insurance are the foundation before any other financial goal.
